title: Jerk chicken
subtitle: Jamaican scotch bonnet and allspice marinated chicken
excerpt: Chicken thighs marinated overnight in a fiery paste of scotch bonnet, allspice, thyme, and garlic, then grilled until charred and deeply fragrant. The defining dish of Jamaica. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Jerk cooking originates with the Maroons (escaped enslaved people) of Jamaica, who preserved and cooked wild pig using indigenous and African spicing techniques in the Blue Mountains. The allspice-scotch bonnet combination is documented by Enid Donaldson in The Real Taste of Jamaica (1993) and by Helen Willinsky in Jerk: Barbecue from Jamaica (1990).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate overnight — 8 hours minimum. The flavour deepens considerably with time.

content.text: The scotch bonnet chilli is very hot and carries a distinctive fruity flavour that makes jerk what it is. Handle with care — wash your hands immediately after touching it and do not rub your eyes. The heat can be reduced by removing the seeds and white membrane before blending, but eliminating the chilli entirely removes the point of the dish. Start with half a scotch bonnet if uncertain.

Blend all the marinade ingredients together in a food processor to a rough paste. Score the chicken thighs deeply with a knife. Rub the paste all over the chicken and into the cuts. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours, or overnight. To cook, heat the grill to its maximum setting. Place the chicken on a foil-lined roasting tray. Grill for 15 minutes skin side down, then turn and grill for 15–20 minutes more until cooked through and charred at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with rice and peas and lime wedges.

items.cause: Scores not deep enough, or marinade time too short.
items.fix: Score all the way to the bone. The overnight rest is what carries the flavour into the meat; a one-hour marinade makes a surface treatment, not a jerk.

items.symptom: Skin burning before chicken is cooked through
items.cause: Grill too hot and chicken placed too close to the element.
items.fix: If the skin chars too fast, move the rack down one level and continue cooking. The chicken is done when the juices run clear at the thickest point.
Maximum heat is essential. The char is not burning — the sugars in the marinade are caramelising to form the crust that gives jerk its flavour.

content.text: Jerk cooking began in the mountains of Jamaica with the Maroon communities, who smoked wild boar and game over pimento wood — pimento being the Jamaican name for the allspice tree, native to the island and grown there commercially. The technique moved into Jamaican street food, cooked over charcoal in halved oil drums fitted with grills, and from there into the diaspora and the wider global food culture. The combination of allspice and scotch bonnet is unique to Jamaica; no other cuisine has produced quite this flavour combination, and the dish that results from it is immediately recognisable.
